title: [PyCDE] Initial cocotb integration
body:
This commit represents a starting point for PyCDE+cocotb integration.
Currently, only iverilog is supported, seeing as verilator has questionable cocotb support (https://github.com/cocotb/cocotb/issues/2300), with questa posing another set of challenges locally*.
Given the limited capabilities, the support is hence mostly intended for PyCDE integration testing.

The "support" consists of syntactic sugar that moves things around to make PyCDE code fit into a cocotb flow.
The `@testbench` decorator defines a testbench on a PyCDE module. Within the testbench class, `@pycdetest` functions define individual tests. These are expected to take a single argument representing the top-level ports of the PyCDE module under test.
This function may then contain any cocotb-style code.

Under the hood, `@testbench` will:
1. Run the PyCDE flow, generating SV file(s).
2. Verbatum copy the `@pycdetest` functions to a separate file, rewriting these to conform to cocotb.
3. Create a makefile for driving the cocotb flow.
4. Do simulator-specific fixup of the SV file(s)**.
5. Run `make` and let cocotb do its thing.

***:** Locally, Questa runs on Windows, cocotb expects to be built within an msys2 environment on Windows, and I've yet to get Python in msys2 to work correctly with the MLIR Python bindings.
****:** While icarus verilog is the simulator that i've managed to push the furthest through this flow, it meets limitations fairly quickly. Some of these can be hackishly overcome by doing some small rewrites of the generated sv file.
